content.full_text:
May 26, 1978

Mr. Larry Schwartz, President
National Order Systems, Inc.
145 W. 58th Street
New York, New York 10019

Dear Mr. Schwartz:

In your letter of May 16, 1978, you ask whether the California
Political Reform Act prohibits or limits fundraising activity in
which donors charge political contributions to their credit card
accounts by telephone. As I understand the system a political
commercial containing a toll-free number is promoted to a tele-
vision audience. A viewer who wishes to make a contribution calls
the number and is connected to your staff. You record the amount
of the contribution and the donor's credit card number and forward
the information to the candidate's bank, which credits the candidates
account in the amount of the contribution. The credit card company
then bills its client-donor.

First, it does not appear to me that the system described above is
prohibited by the Political Reform Act. However, a number of the
requirements of the Act will have special implications for your
operations.

California Government Code §84300 requires that, "Any contribution
of fifty dollars or more other than an in-kind contribution shall
be made by a written instrument containing the name of the donor and
the name of the payee." To comply with this Section it would be
necessary in every case that a copy of the credit card voucher be
sent to the recipient of the contribution as soon as practicable
after the donor's instructions are received by you.

California Government Code §84209(g) specifies the information
required to be contained in disclosure reports filed periodically
by candidates and committees. For each person from whom a contri-
bution or contributions of fifty dollars or more has been received
the recipient must report the donor's full name, street address,
occupation, employer's name or the principal place of business if
self-employed, the amount contributed, the date on which each con-
tribution was received and the cumulative amount contributed by
the donor. For purposes of determining whether the $50 threshold
has been reached the cumulative amount governs.

California Government Code §84302 provides that anyone making a
contribution on behalf of another or while acting as the intermediary
or agent of another must disclose to the recipient of the contribution
both the intermediary's or agent's full name and street address,
occupation, employer's name or principal place of business if self-
employed, and the full name and street address, occupation, employer's

Mr. Larry Schwartz, President
May 26, 1978
Page Two

name or principal place of business if self-employed of the ultimate
donor. Under the Section a applied to the fundraising scheme you
propose, it would be your responsibility to collect the stipulated
information from the donors and transmit it to the recipients.

California Government Code §84100(c) requires that all contributions
received by a person acting as an agent of a Candidate shall be re-
ported promptly by such person to the candidate or any of his
designated agents. All contributions received by a person acting as
an agent of a committee shall be reported promptly by the recipient
to the committee's treasurer or any of his designated agents.
"Promptly" means not later than five days before the closing date of
any campaign statement required to be filed by the treasurer and
immediately if the contributions is received less than five days
before the closing date.

The above Sections are not an exhaustive list of the requirements of
the Act that may be applicable to your proposed operations. A copy
of the Political Reform Act is enclosed. If you have further
questions, you may call me at (916) 442-0821.

Sincerely,


John D. Giansello
Political Reform Analyst
Technical Assistance and
Analysis Division

Enclosure

EPPO

MAY 22 1978
Office of the Secretary of State
March Fong Eu

111 Capitol Mall, Rm 200
P.O. Box 1467
Sacramento, California 95807

POLITICAL REFORM DIVISION
(916) 322-4880

May 19, 1978


Mr. Larry Schwartz, President
National Order Systems, Inc.
145 West 58th Street
New York, NY 10019

Dear Mr. Schwartz:

Thank you for your letter of May 16, 1978, regarding your
toll-free answering service which receives pledges and
contributions via telephone.

Your letter has been forwarded for response to the Fair
Political Practices Commission, P.O. Box 807, Sacramento
California 95804, telephone number (916) 322-5662. The
Commission, an independent agency established by California's Political Reform Act, has the primary responsibility
for administering, implementing, and explaining the provisions of the campaign disclosure law.

If our office may provide any assistance to you in the
future, do not hesitate to contact me.

Sincerely,

WILLIAM N. DURLEY
Assistant to the Secretary of State
Elections and Political Reform

JANET E. SANDLIN, Auditor
Political Reform Division

cc: -Fair Political Practices Commission

NATIONAL ORDER SYSTEMS, INC. Toll-Free Telephone Marketing Services
145 W. 58th Street, New York, N.Y. 10019 • (212) 245-2250

May 16, 1978


Secretary of State
State Capital Building
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Sir:

I am writing to inquire whether the laws and regulations
of your department and your state permit political con-
tributions to be charged to credit cards.

My company is a nation-wide toll-free answering service
which receives pledges and contributions of all kinds
via toll-free phone, resulting from advertising on radio
and TV.

We have had inquiries from candidates in many states
regarding this service, and are researching the
regulations of each state in this manner so that we
know which course of action to follow in handling
political contributions which are phoned in.

I should make it clear that we have not yet received
inquiries from any candidates in your state, but since
we have received inquiries from so many other states,
we want to have the regulations of all 50 states in our
files. In this way we will be prepared for candidates
from your state as soon as we hear from them regarding
our service.

Assuming that your regulations permit charging contributions
to credit cards, the sequence works as follows: the political
commercial appeals on the air and shows the toll-free number
which viewers can call to make their contributions. We receive
their call, take the amount of their contribution and the
number of their credit card. We then forward this information
to the candidate's bank, where it is deposited in his campaign
account.

In the event that your laws do not permit the above activity,
we simply send invoices to all callers who make pledges.

- 2 -

We will appreciate hearing from you at your earliest
convenience as to whether the credit card sequence is
permissible in your state.

Please call me if you need any further information.

Thank you.

Sincerely,

Larry Schwartz
Larry Schwartz
President

LS:dc
